A 40th birthday expedition down the San Lorenzo

Wading Through the Garden of Eden

We walk into the river beneath the redwoods. Three miles later we walk out, climb the canyon wall, and go find a long dinner and a proper toast. Kai grew up in California rivers; for the 40th, he's finally following this one all the way down.

Saturday, August 29, 2026 · optional Sunday brunch · bring water shoes
~3 mi
in the river
4-5 hrs
wading + swimming
3 holes
worth jumping in
½ mi
climb out (~250 ft)

The idea

Kai spent his childhood summers in California rivers, and this one is now home water: the beach by the Henry Cowell picnic area is where he brings his own kids. But every family visit ends the same way, with the river bending out of sight downstream, unexplored. This time we follow it. No trail, no signage, no opinions from the internet: the water runs ankle-to-knee deep in late August with a few chest-deep pools, the canyon does the navigating, and every 45 minutes it hands us a swimming hole. We exit where the park ends, because the next thing downstream is a private Masonic community and they are not expecting us.

The wade, artist's impression.

Plan A: the wade If it's sunny

  1. 11:45Meet at Kai's place, San Jose. Coffee, sunscreen, water-shoe inspection.
  2. 12:00Uber XL to Felton. Drop-off: Henry Cowell Redwoods State Park visitor center, 101 N Big Trees Park Rd (~45 min via Hwy 17).
  3. 12:50Arrive, gear up. Bathrooms and water fountains here, the last reliable ones until dinner. Peek at the old-growth grove if we're quick.
  4. 13:15Feet in the water at the river beach by the picnic area. Wade under the Roaring Camp railroad trestle and into the canyon.
  5. 14:30Garden of Eden. The famous one: deep blue-green pool in a rock cove. Long swim stop, snacks, and the birthday toast on the river.
  6. 15:45Cable Car Beach. Sandy, sunny, usually quieter. Lounge accordingly.
  7. 16:30Big Rock Hole. A big rock, a deep hole. Feet-first jumps, sand beach, last swim.
  8. 17:15Exit at Rincon. From Big Rock Hole, Rincon Fire Road climbs a short, steep half-mile (~250 ft) straight up to Highway 9. The price of the day, and it's a fair one.
  9. 17:45Pickup at the Rincon parking lot on Highway 9. Pre-booked van (details below). Change into dry shirts, feel superior.

Times are an example schedule, not a contract. The river sets the pace; everything after 13:15 is approximate.

Garden of Eden, artist's impression.

The map

The wade (Plan A) Exit climb to pickup Redwood hike (Plan B)

Route lines follow the river and trail as mapped in OpenStreetMap, simplified for readability; treat them as orientation, not navigation. The river itself is the trail. Screenshot this map before leaving San Jose; there is little to no cell signal in the canyon.

Drop-off pin (Felton) Pickup pin (Rincon lot) Live river flow (USGS)

Plan B: the redwood hike If it's chilly

The water is ~60°F either way; sunshine is what makes the wade great. If the Felton forecast for our Saturday shows a high under about 70°F or stubborn overcast, we keep boots on and hike the same park instead. Same drop-off, same pickup, same dinner. Nobody rebooks anything.

  • Redwood Grove Loop first: the flat, famous 0.8 mile through old growth, including a 1,500-year-old giant you can stand inside.
  • Climb to the Observation Deck via River, Eagle Creek, and Pine trails: ponderosa pines growing out of ancient sea-floor sand, and on a clear day a line of ocean on the horizon. Picnic at the top.
  • Descend Rincon Fire Road to the river crossing for a look at Big Rock Hole. Optional feet-dip for the brave, then back up to the Rincon lot for the same 17:45 pickup.
  • Totals: roughly 5 miles, 700-800 ft of gain, 3.5-4.5 hours at a conversational pace.

Plan B: the old growth up top.

The call gets made Thursday before. Check the forecast for Felton, not Santa Cruz: the valley usually burns off the fog by noon and runs 5-10°F warmer than the coast, so Plan A survives most "meh" coastal forecasts.

The evening: Santa Cruz

  1. 17:45Van from Rincon lot straight to the Dream Inn (~12 min). No transfers, no downtown detour.
  2. 18:15Dinner at Jack O'Neill Restaurant, on the sand at Cowell Beach: floor-to-ceiling glass over the bay and the wharf, calm enough for one conversation, and sunset (~19:45 in late August) lands mid-meal. The proper birthday toast happens here. Reservation and a window-table request go in once headcount locks.
  3. 20:30Optional round two, no ride needed: drinks on the Jack O'Neill Lounge deck over the beach (Santa Cruz Mountains wine, Venus Spirits cocktails), or a night stroll out the wharf toward the Boardwalk lights.
  4. 21:45Uber XL home to San Jose from the Dream Inn: a proper address with good signal, the easiest pickup of the day. Home ~22:45.
Backups, if Jack O'Neill can't take us:
  • Laili (downtown): Afghan-Mediterranean in a secluded courtyard built for conversation, food built for sharing. Gotcha: evenings run cool out there.
  • Gabriella Café (downtown): intimate Italian in a 1920s building on a quiet side street. Gotcha: small room, so a group needs an early booking.
  • Johnny's Harborside (harbor): second-floor windows over the boat docks, mellow room, twenty-year local. Gotcha: less walkable for anything after.

The morning after Optional

  • Crash at Kai's. We can host 2-3 overnight in San Jose; first text claims a spot. Everyone else Ubers home Saturday night as planned.
  • Sunday brunch, August 30. Late morning at Kai's for whoever stayed, plus anyone who wants to wander back. Pancakes, coffee, and a full replay of the jump footage.
  • Overnight kit: toothbrush and a change of clothes. The rest is provided.

Logistics, including the one hard problem

The hard problem: cell service. Coverage is poor from the trestle to the Rincon lot, and spotty at the lot itself. You cannot count on hailing an Uber from the exit. So we do not try:
  • Primary: pre-booked local taxi or van, arranged days ahead from home for "Rincon parking lot, Highway 9, 17:45." Driver shows up whether or not we have bars.
  • Backup: Uber Reserve, scheduled for the same time and place. Whichever ride we don't need gets cancelled once someone finds signal.
  • Floor: our own feet. If nothing has wheels by 18:05, we walk downhill along Highway 9 toward town; signal typically returns within a mile or two near the golf course, and downtown is only 3 miles. Annoying, survivable, still ends in beer.
  • Group rules: phones in dry bags and mostly useless all afternoon. Screenshot this page and the map. Tell partners we're off-grid 13:00-18:00.
  • Ride costs (estimates only, surge varies): Uber XL San Jose to Felton $65-85; pickup van to Santa Cruz $40-60; Uber XL Santa Cruz to San Jose at night $75-100. Split across the crew it lands around $35-50 each for the whole day's transport (estimate, depends on headcount).
  • Park entry: the $10 day-use fee is per vehicle; arriving by Uber, we may owe nothing. Bring a little cash regardless.
  • Packing note: alcohol and glass are banned inside the park (rangers do ticket), so the river toast travels in water bottles and the real pour waits for dinner.
  • Weather and water check: Felton forecast plus the USGS Big Trees gauge. Late August typically runs 15-20 cfs, which is ideal wading. If a freak storm has it above ~50 cfs, we default to Plan B.

What to bring

  • Water shoes or old trainers with real grip. The single most important item. No flip-flops.
  • Swim trunks and a quick-dry shirt (long sun-exposed stretches).
  • Small backpack with everything in a dry bag or doubled ziplocks: phone, card, cash.
  • Dry shirt, shorts, and socks for dinner. Future-you says thanks.
  • 1.5-2 liters of water each, plus salty snacks.
  • Sunscreen, hat, sunglasses with a strap.
  • Optional: compact towel, waterproof phone pouch, a found stick for the deep bits.
  • Kai brings: first aid kit, spare ziplocks, birthday snacks. Zero glass.
Hold Saturday, August 29.

Invites coming. Overnight spots and Sunday brunch (August 30) go to whoever claims them first.
Weather call goes out the Thursday before. Water shoes, gentlemen.
